rank_1_reason: Broad‑spectrum efficacy covering most seizure types, already in use, inexpensive and aligns with Uganda’s first‑line guideline; no pregnancy or hepatic contraindications in this pre‑pubertal child.
rank_2_reason: Broad‑spectrum, rapid titration, relatively low cost for a newer agent, and a different mechanism from VPA, making it a solid backup if VPA fails.
rank_3_reason: Effective for both focal and generalized seizures, inexpensive, and well‑tolerated; chosen as a third option despite the need for slow titration because it avoids the cognitive side effects of TPM and the sedation of benzodiazepines.

rank_1_reason: Already providing good control (only one seizure since initiation) and is the first‑line broad‑spectrum agent for unknown seizure types in this age group; continued use is preferred over switching.
rank_2_reason: Broad‑spectrum, inexpensive, rapid onset without need for slow titration; an effective alternative if VPA tolerance issues arise.
rank_3_reason: Very low cost, effective across seizure types, and remains a reliable backup in resource‑limited settings despite its sedating profile.

rank_1_reason: First‑line, inexpensive oral agent with proven high efficacy for generalized tonic‑clonic seizures; suitable for a 5‑year‑old without hepatic or pregnancy concerns.
rank_2_reason: Broad‑spectrum, well‑tolerated, inexpensive, and can be started quickly; a good alternative if VPA is poorly tolerated or contraindicated.
rank_3_reason: Effective for GTCS and also covers focal components; usable if both VPA and LEV are unsuitable, though it requires gradual titration to minimize rash risk.

rank_1_reason: First‑line broad‑spectrum drug with proven efficacy for myoclonic‑astatic (Doose) seizures and readily available at low cost in Uganda.
rank_2_reason: Effective broad‑spectrum alternative that avoids VPA’s teratogenic risk and can be initiated quickly without slow titration.
rank_3_reason: Very inexpensive and historically used for generalized seizures; serves as a fallback option when VPA or LEV are unavailable or not tolerated, despite its sedating profile.

rank_1_reason: Broad‑spectrum, inexpensive, and effective for multifocal/focal seizures; no hepatic or pregnancy contraindication in this 2‑year‑old, making it the best single‑agent switch from the inadequate carbamazepine.
rank_2_reason: Rapid‑onset, broad‑spectrum agent with a different mechanism; suitable as an alternative monotherapy or as adjunct to valproate if further seizure control is needed, and its side‑effect profile is acceptable for this age.
rank_3_reason: Very low‑cost, established efficacy for focal seizures; kept as a fallback option despite sedation risk, useful when newer agents are unavailable or not tolerated.
